title: Pet Sematary - The Ran(King) of King - Part 6
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ezrw0VHCy7w

Daniel (@dungeondive) reaches 1983's Pet Sematary in The Ran(King) of King — one of the earliest King books he read (around seventh grade), and one whose images never left him. He frames it as a sustained meditation on death, the one force binding all living things, present from the very first line. It's King's bleakest book by his own admission — so gruesome and hopeless that he shelved the finished manuscript, horrified, and published Christine first.

Dr. Louis Creed moves his family to rural Maine, near a pet cemetery behind which lies an ancient Micmac burial ground with the power to return the dead — to terrible result. Daniel praises King's evident burial-and-funeral research, which lends the prose real confidence, and argues the book's true power lies less in death itself than in the *relationships* around it: the father-son friendship between Louis and his neighbour Jud (which recalls his own grandfather's storytelling), and the deeply true marriage of Louis and Rachel (he reads a remarkable passage on the unknowable separateness of another mind). The undead horror only arrives in the final fifth — a slow burn that makes everything more meaningful.

His verdict: King's best straight horror novel. It takes first place on the board.

----

Is Pet Sematary the King horror novel you'd hand a newcomer — and does its slow-burn, relationship-driven dread land harder than gore would?
